questions.text: What type of attack floods a target with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users?

answers.text: Phishing
SQL Injection
Denial of Service (DoS)
Man-in-the-Middle

explanations.a: Phishing deceives users into revealing credentials via fake emails or websites.
explanations.b: SQL Injection manipulates database queries through unsanitized user input.
explanations.c: DoS/DDoS attacks overwhelm a target with traffic, exhausting its resources. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: MitM intercepts communications between two parties, potentially altering them.

answers.text: A brute-force attack on passwords
A social engineering attack that tricks users into revealing sensitive information
Malware that encrypts files and demands a ransom
An attack that exploits unpatched software vulnerabilities

explanations.a: Brute-force attacks systematically try password combinations — that is credential stuffing or password spraying.
explanations.b: Phishing uses deceptive emails or fake sites to steal credentials or install malware. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Encrypting files for ransom describes ransomware.
explanations.d: Exploiting unpatched vulnerabilities describes an exploit attack or zero-day attack.

answers.text: Software that spies on user activity
Malware that encrypts victim data and demands payment for decryption
A virus that deletes system files
A worm that spreads through email attachments

explanations.a: Software that spies on user activity is called spyware.
explanations.b: Ransomware encrypts data and extorts the victim for a decryption key. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: File-deleting malware is typically a wiper or destructive virus.
explanations.d: Email-spreading worms are a separate malware category, though ransomware can be delivered via email.

questions.text: What distinguishes a zero-day vulnerability from other vulnerabilities?

answers.text: It can only be exploited on day zero of software release
It is publicly known but has no patch available yet
It is unknown to the software vendor and has no patch
It requires physical access to exploit

explanations.a: Zero-day does not literally mean day zero of release — it refers to vendor awareness.
explanations.b: A vulnerability that is public but unpatched is an n-day vulnerability, not strictly a zero-day.
explanations.c: A zero-day is unknown to the vendor, giving them zero days to prepare a fix. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Zero-days can be exploited remotely; physical access is not a requirement.

answers.text: A method to speed up database queries
Inserting malicious SQL statements into an input field to manipulate a database
A type of virus that infects SQL Server installations
A backup strategy for SQL databases

explanations.a: Query optimization is a performance concern unrelated to SQL injection.
explanations.b: SQL injection exploits unsanitized input to execute unauthorized database commands. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: SQL injection is an attack technique, not a virus.
explanations.d: Backup strategies are a separate database administration topic.

answers.text: An attacker who physically sits between two computers
An attack where the adversary secretly intercepts and possibly alters communications between two parties
A phishing attack targeting executives
An attack that exploits misconfigured middleware software

explanations.a: MitM is a logical interception — physical proximity is not required.
explanations.b: In a MitM attack, the adversary secretly relays and potentially alters messages between two communicating parties. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Phishing targeting executives is called whaling or spear phishing.
explanations.d: Middleware misconfiguration is a separate application security issue.

questions.text: What is the difference between a virus and a worm?

answers.text: Viruses encrypt files; worms steal data
Viruses require a host file and user action to spread; worms self-replicate across networks without user interaction
Worms require a host file; viruses spread autonomously
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable

explanations.a: File encryption is a characteristic of ransomware, not the virus/worm distinction.
explanations.b: Viruses attach to host files and need user execution; worms propagate independently over networks. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: This reverses the definitions — viruses need hosts, worms do not.
explanations.d: Virus and worm describe distinct propagation behaviors and are not interchangeable.

questions.text: What is an Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)?

answers.text: A single automated attack using multiple exploit kits
A prolonged, targeted cyberattack in which an intruder remains undetected over an extended period
A DDoS attack that lasts longer than 24 hours
Malware that persists after a system reboot

explanations.a: APTs are typically human-operated campaigns, not just automated exploit kits.
explanations.b: APTs are sophisticated, long-term intrusions — often state-sponsored — aimed at data theft or sabotage. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: APT is not defined by DDoS duration; it refers to stealthy, targeted campaigns.
explanations.d: Persistence mechanisms are a tactic within an APT, but an APT encompasses a full campaign.

answers.text: An attack that injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
A method for sharing scripts between different websites
An attack that forges HTTP requests on behalf of a logged-in user
A technique to bypass same-origin policy by modifying DNS

explanations.a: XSS injects client-side scripts into pages, executing in the victim's browser to steal session tokens or perform actions. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Script sharing between sites is a development practice unrelated to XSS.
explanations.c: Forging authenticated requests describes CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery).
explanations.d: DNS manipulation to bypass same-origin policy describes DNS rebinding, not XSS.
